infobox.value: (spoken "four-inch-fifty-caliber") was the standard low-angle, [quick-firing gun](/wiki/Quick-firing_gun "Quick-firing gun") for the United States, first appearing on the [monitor](/wiki/Monitor_(warship) "Monitor (warship)") [*Arkansas*](/wiki/USS_Arkansas_(BM-7) "USS Arkansas (BM-7)") and then used on "Flush Deck" [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyers "Destroyers") through [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and the 1920s. It was also the standard [deck gun](/wiki/Deck_gun "Deck gun") on [S-class](/wiki/United_States_S-class_submarine "United States S-class submarine") submarines, and was used to rearm numerous submarines built with 3-inch (76 mm) guns early in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). United States naval gun terminology indicates the gun fired a projectile 4 inches (10 centimeters) in diameter, and the barrel was 50 calibers long. 4 inches x50 meant the barrel was 200 inches long, or 16 feet long .[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-friedman-3)

The **4″/50-caliber gun** (spoken "four-inch-fifty-caliber") was the standard low-angle, [quick-firing gun](/wiki/Quick-firing_gun "Quick-firing gun") for the United States, first appearing on the [monitor](/wiki/Monitor_(warship) "Monitor (warship)") [*Arkansas*](/wiki/USS_Arkansas_(BM-7) "USS Arkansas (BM-7)") and then used on "Flush Deck" [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyers "Destroyers") through [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and the 1920s. It was also the standard [deck gun](/wiki/Deck_gun "Deck gun") on [S-class](/wiki/United_States_S-class_submarine "United States S-class submarine") submarines, and was used to rearm numerous submarines built with 3-inch (76 mm) guns early in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). United States naval gun terminology indicates the gun fired a projectile 4 inches (10 centimeters) in diameter, and the barrel was 50 calibers long. 4 inches x50 meant the barrel was 200 inches long, or 16 feet long .[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-friedman-3)

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4-inch/50-caliber_gun&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Design")]

The original 4-inch/50-caliber Mark 7 gun, **M1898**, serial nos. 213–254, 257–281, 316–338, was an entirely new high-power design [built-up gun](/wiki/Built-up_gun "Built-up gun") with a tube, jacket, [hoop](/wiki/Hoop_gun "Hoop gun"), locking ring and screw breech. Gun No. 213 had a liner. The gun was described as a 5-inch (127 mm) gun but with a 4-inch bore in the 1902 handbook, this indicated its higher power and also the fact the barrel was actually more the size of a [5-inch/40 caliber gun](/wiki/5%22/40_caliber_gun "5\"/40 caliber gun") than a 4-inch gun. The ammunition was about 7 lb (3.2 kg) heavier than a [4-inch/40 caliber](/wiki/4%22/40_caliber_gun "4\"/40 caliber gun") round. The Mod 1 was a Mod 0 that used a conical steel liner and the Mod 2 was either a Mod 0 or Mod 1 that was relined using a conical [nickel-steel](/wiki/Nickel-steel "Nickel-steel") liner and a shoulder on the breech end.[[3]](#cite_note-friedman-3)

Gun No. 353 was the prototype of the Mark 8 and was test fired on 22 September 1910. This gun had been ordered 16 June 1907 and delivered in November 1907. The simplified design of the Mark 8 had just a gun tube and jacket. The jacket extended all the way to the muzzle and ended in a muzzle bell. The production run was small with only 12 guns built, Nos. 353–364.[[3]](#cite_note-friedman-3)

The Mark 9 was a design directly resulting from tests with gun No. 353. It was designed to be light in weight, and would go on to be the standard 4-inch gun used on destroyers and submarines during WW I. The gun would use an A tube, full-length jacket, a muzzle swell with a side swing [Smith-Asbury breech mechanism](/wiki/Smith-Asbury_mechanism "Smith-Asbury mechanism") and [Welin breech block](/wiki/Welin_breech_block "Welin breech block"). The gun weighed about 2.725 short tons (2.472 t). Gun No. 365, the first Mark 9, was ordered from [Midvale Steel](/wiki/Midvale_Steel "Midvale Steel") on 18 October 1911. There were 390 Mark 9s built by four different manufacturers from 1911 until the US entered World War I in 1917. During the war another 1,885 guns were produced, with [Root & VanDervoort](/w/index.php?title=Root_%26_VanDervoort&action=edit&redlink=1 "Root & VanDervoort (page does not exist)"), [American Radiator Company](/wiki/American_Radiator_Company "American Radiator Company") and [Poole Engineering](/w/index.php?title=Poole_Engineering&action=edit&redlink=1 "Poole Engineering (page does not exist)") joining the pre-war manufacturers. After the Armistice another 713 guns were produced, with orders for 3538 guns cancelled. It was decided after World War I that all destroyers would carry the 4-inch/50-caliber Mark 9 Mod 5 gun; the refits were completed in autumn 1921.[[3]](#cite_note-friedman-3)

The Mark 10, gun No. 365-A, was ordered in 1915 but does not appear to have been completed until after WW I. The initial drawings were for a 4-inch/50-caliber [anti-aircraft](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_warfare "Anti-aircraft warfare") gun dated January and February 1915. It was designed with a vertically sliding breech block on a built-up gun with a tube, jacket, chase hoop and locking ring, all constructed of nickel steel, but it does not appear that the Mark 10 was put into service.[[3]](#cite_note-friedman-3)

The gun was rapid firing (US term) or quick firing (British term). Fixed ammunition (case and projectile handled as a single assembled unit) with a 14.5-pound (6.6 kg) charge of [smokeless powder](/wiki/Smokeless_powder "Smokeless powder") gave a 33-pound (15 kg) projectile a velocity of 2,900 ft/s (880 m/s). Range was 9 mi (14 km) at the maximum elevation of 20 degrees. Useful life expectancy was 400–500 [effective full charges](/wiki/List_of_British_ordnance_terms#Effective_Full_Charge "List of British ordnance terms") (EFC) for a non-chrome plated barrel, while a chrome plated barrel was listed at 600 rounds.[[1]](#cite_note-Campbell_1985_p.143-1)[[3]](#cite_note-friedman-3)

Increasing awareness of the need for improved [anti-aircraft](/wiki/Anti-aircraft "Anti-aircraft") protection resulted in the mounting of [dual purpose guns](/wiki/Dual_purpose_gun "Dual purpose gun") on destroyers beginning in the 1930s. The dual-purpose [5-inch/38 caliber gun](/wiki/5%22/38_caliber_gun "5\"/38 caliber gun") became standard for United States destroyers constructed from the 1930s through [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). United States destroyers built with 4-inch/50-caliber low-angle guns were mostly rearmed with dual-purpose [3-inch/50 caliber guns](/wiki/3%22/50_caliber_gun "3\"/50 caliber gun") during the war. The 4-inch/50-caliber guns removed from destroyers were mounted on [Defensively Equipped Merchant Ships](/wiki/Defensively_Equipped_Merchant_Ships "Defensively Equipped Merchant Ships") of the [British Merchant Navy](/wiki/Merchant_Navy_(United_Kingdom) "Merchant Navy (United Kingdom)") and [United States Merchant Marine](/wiki/United_States_Merchant_Marine "United States Merchant Marine") like [SS *Stephen Hopkins*](/wiki/Sinking_of_the_SS_Stephen_Hopkins "Sinking of the SS Stephen Hopkins").[[1]](#cite_note-Campbell_1985_p.143-1) As [S-boats](/wiki/United_States_S-class_submarine "United States S-class submarine") were transferred from combat patrols to training duties from mid-1942 through 1943, their 4-inch guns were removed and used to re-equip front-line submarines built with 3-inch/50-caliber guns.

## Manufacturer list Mark 9 gun

The unassigned numbers mostly corresponded to gun orders that were cancelled with the signing of the Armistice.[[3]](#cite_note-friedman-3)

## US Navy service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4-inch/50-caliber_gun&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: US Navy service")]

The 4″/50-caliber gun was mounted on:

* [*Arkansas*-class monitors](/wiki/Arkansas-class_monitor "Arkansas-class monitor") (Mark 7)
* [*Cassin*-class destroyers](/wiki/Cassin-class_destroyer "Cassin-class destroyer")[[4]](#cite_note-GardinerWWIUSDD-4)
* [*Aylwin*-class destroyers](/wiki/Aylwin-class_destroyer "Aylwin-class destroyer")
* [*O'Brien*-class destroyers](/wiki/O%27Brien-class_destroyer "O'Brien-class destroyer")[[4]](#cite_note-GardinerWWIUSDD-4)
* [*Tucker*-class destroyers](/wiki/Tucker-class_destroyer "Tucker-class destroyer")[[4]](#cite_note-GardinerWWIUSDD-4)
* [*Sampson*-class destroyers](/wiki/Sampson-class_destroyer "Sampson-class destroyer")[[4]](#cite_note-GardinerWWIUSDD-4)
* [*Caldwell*-class destroyers](/wiki/Caldwell-class_destroyer "Caldwell-class destroyer")[[5]](#cite_note-Fahey_1939_p.14-5)
* [*Wickes*-class destroyers](/wiki/Wickes-class_destroyer "Wickes-class destroyer")[[5]](#cite_note-Fahey_1939_p.14-5)
* [*Clemson*-class destroyers](/wiki/Clemson-class_destroyer "Clemson-class destroyer")[[5]](#cite_note-Fahey_1939_p.14-5)
* [United States S-class submarines](/wiki/United_States_S-class_submarine "United States S-class submarine")[[6]](#cite_note-Fahey_1939_p.18-6)
* The first seven [*Balao*-class submarines](/wiki/Balao-class_submarine "Balao-class submarine")[[1]](#cite_note-Campbell_1985_p.143-1)
* [USS *Dolphin*](/wiki/USS_Dolphin_(SS-169) "USS Dolphin (SS-169)")[[6]](#cite_note-Fahey_1939_p.18-6)
* Numerous rearmed submarines including [USS *Salmon*](/wiki/USS_Salmon_(SS-182) "USS Salmon (SS-182)"), [USS *Seadragon*](/wiki/USS_Seadragon_(SS-194) "USS Seadragon (SS-194)"), [USS *Gato*](/wiki/USS_Gato_(SS-212) "USS Gato (SS-212)"), [USS *Silversides*](/wiki/USS_Silversides_(SS-236) "USS Silversides (SS-236)") and [USS *Robalo*](/wiki/USS_Robalo "USS Robalo")[[1]](#cite_note-Campbell_1985_p.143-1)
* [Eagle-class patrol craft](/wiki/Eagle-class_patrol_craft "Eagle-class patrol craft")
* Some minesweepers - Q-ships like [USS *Eagle*](/wiki/USS_Captor "USS Captor")
* Some minelayers like [USS *Miantonomah*](/wiki/USS_Miantonomah_(CMc-5) "USS Miantonomah (CMc-5)")
* Some patrol gunboats like [USS *Sacramento*](/wiki/USS_Sacramento_(PG-19) "USS Sacramento (PG-19)"), [*Asheville*-class gunboat](/wiki/Asheville-class_gunboat_(1917) "Asheville-class gunboat (1917)"), [USS *Plymouth*](/wiki/USS_Plymouth_(PG-57) "USS Plymouth (PG-57)")
* armed yachts,

## United States Merchant Marine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4-inch/50-caliber_gun&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: United States Merchant Marine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/4inch_50caliber_gun_on_SS_Sommelsdijk_80-G-0050053_001.jpg/250px-4inch_50caliber_gun_on_SS_Sommelsdijk_80-G-0050053_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:4inch_50caliber_gun_on_SS_Sommelsdijk_80-G-0050053_001.jpg)

4-inch/50-caliber gun on merchant ship SS Sommelsdijk

* Most [Liberty ships](/wiki/Liberty_ship "Liberty ship")
* World War I [underway replenishment oilers](/wiki/Underway_replenishment "Underway replenishment") like [USS *Maumee*](/wiki/USS_Maumee_(AO-2) "USS Maumee (AO-2)")
* Some World War II oilers like [USS *Big Horn*](/wiki/USS_Big_Horn_(AO-45) "USS Big Horn (AO-45)"), [USS *Victoria*](/wiki/USS_Victoria_(AO-46) "USS Victoria (AO-46)") and [USS *Pasig*](/wiki/USS_Pasig_(AO-89) "USS Pasig (AO-89)")

## Coast defense use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4-inch/50-caliber_gun&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Coast defense use")]

Four two-gun batteries of 4″/50-caliber ex-Navy guns were emplaced on the North Shore of Oahu in 1942. They seem to have been withdrawn in 1943 as other defenses were constructed. It is not clear who operated these guns; likely possibilities include the [United States Army Coast Artillery Corps](/wiki/United_States_Army_Coast_Artillery_Corps "United States Army Coast Artillery Corps"), [Marine defense battalions](/wiki/Marine_defense_battalions "Marine defense battalions"), or naval personnel. The batteries were at Kaena, Kalihi (Mokuoeo Island), Battery Dillingham at Mokuleia, and Kaneohe Bay.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## UK service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=4-inch/50-caliber_gun&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: UK service")]

Many Mark 9 guns were supplied to the United Kingdom during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") as part of [Lend-lease](/wiki/Lend-lease "Lend-lease"), both individually and on naval and merchant ships.[[8]](#cite_note-8) *Caldwell*, *Wickes*, and *Clemson*-class destroyers transferred under the [Destroyers for Bases Agreement](/wiki/Destroyers_for_Bases_Agreement "Destroyers for Bases Agreement") became British and Canadian [Town-class destroyers](/wiki/Town-class_destroyer "Town-class destroyer").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
